1 - Lagging: Test execution is severely backlogged; QA is the bottleneck in every sprint. Test cases pile up and releases are delayed waiting on QA sign-off.
2 - Developing: Test cycles complete but often spill past sprint boundaries. The team of 14 is busy but throughput doesn't match capacity; idle time between assignments is common.
3 - Proficient: Consistent test cycle completion within sprint timelines. Both web and mobile test suites are executed on schedule with clear pass/fail reporting via ClickUp.
4 - Exemplary: QA throughput exceeds sprint demand; the team proactively pulls in regression and exploratory testing. Automated test suites handle bulk execution, freeing the team for high-value exploratory work.

1 - Lagging: Test estimates are consistently wrong; scope of testing is unclear until mid-sprint. QA is always scrambling at release time.
2 - Developing: Test plans exist but are loosely scoped; edge cases are frequently missed, causing last-minute test additions that delay releases.
3 - Proficient: 80%+ of planned test cases are executed on time. Test plans are well-scoped with clear entry/exit criteria; coverage is predictable across web and mobile platforms.
4 - Exemplary: Data-driven test estimation using historical defect rates and cycle times. Near-perfect predictability; stakeholders trust QA timelines implicitly.

1 - Lagging: High number of production escapes; critical bugs are found by end-users rather than QA. Bug reports are vague and hard to reproduce.
2 - Developing: Most major bugs are caught pre-release, but edge cases and cross-platform issues (web vs mobile) frequently slip through. Bug reports lack consistent detail.
3 - Proficient: Strong defect capture rate across both web and mobile applications. Bug reports are detailed with steps to reproduce, severity, platform info, and screenshots/videos.
4 - Exemplary: Near-zero production escapes for critical/major severity. QA acts as the last line of defense with comprehensive cross-platform, cross-browser, and device-specific coverage.

1 - Lagging: QA operates in a silo; developers are surprised by bug reports. Blame culture between QA and dev teams is common.
2 - Developing: QA communicates with dev teams but only reactively — flagging issues late in the cycle. Handoffs between QA and Frontend/Backend/Mobile teams are clunky.
3 - Proficient: Active collaboration with all dev teams (Frontend, Backend, Mobile, Hardware, GameDev). QA is embedded in sprint planning and provides early feedback on testability.
4 - Exemplary: QA is a strategic partner across all divisions. Proactively improves the entire company's quality culture; co-designs test strategies with dev leads before development begins.

1 - Lagging: QA engineers are randomly assigned without regard to platform expertise. Senior testers are doing basic smoke tests while juniors struggle with complex flows.
2 - Developing: Some specialization exists (web vs mobile) but assignments are rigid. When one platform has a surge, the other team can't flex to help.
3 - Proficient: Clear specialization tracks (web QA, mobile QA) with cross-training. The team of 14 is allocated based on sprint priorities; skill-to-task mapping is deliberate.
4 - Exemplary: Dynamic allocation; QA engineers shift between web and mobile testing seamlessly based on release priority. Specialists mentor generalists, creating a highly flexible team.

1 - Lagging: Burnout is common during release weeks; 2-3 senior testers carry the team. Workload distribution across 14 members is severely uneven.
2 - Developing: Workload varies significantly; some testers are overloaded while others wait for builds. Bus factor is concerning — key platform knowledge sits with 1-2 people.
3 - Proficient: Predictable workload distribution across the team. No single point of failure; at least 2-3 testers can cover any critical test area. On-call rotation for hotfix testing is fair.
4 - Exemplary: Sustainable high performance; proactive load-balancing using ClickUp capacity views. Cross-trained team means any member can cover any area with minimal ramp-up.

1 - Lagging: Testing is entirely manual; no test management tool integration. Device lab is disorganized; testers waste time setting up environments.
2 - Developing: Basic test tools are in place but lack integration with CI/CD pipelines. Device coverage is incomplete; some mobile devices and browser versions are untested.
3 - Proficient: Well-maintained device lab (physical + cloud-based). Test management integrated with ClickUp. Automated regression suites cover core flows for both web and mobile.
4 - Exemplary: Best-in-class QA infrastructure; comprehensive device farm, parallel test execution, and real-time dashboards showing test coverage, pass rates, and defect trends.

1 - Lagging: Excessive manual testing hours with diminishing returns. No ROI analysis on testing effort vs defect yield.
2 - Developing: Budget is tracked but not optimized; too much time spent on low-risk areas while high-risk areas are under-tested.
3 - Proficient: Risk-based testing approach ensures effort is concentrated where it matters most. Clear correlation between testing investment and production quality metrics.
4 - Exemplary: High ROI on QA investment; automation reduces manual effort significantly. Metrics prove QA saves more in prevented production incidents than it costs to operate.

1 - Lagging: Stakeholders are surprised by quality issues at release time. QA status is a black box to project managers and product owners.
2 - Developing: QA updates are provided only when asked; reports use technical jargon that stakeholders struggle to interpret.
3 - Proficient: Regular, proactive quality status updates. Clear dashboards in ClickUp showing test progress, defect trends, and release readiness across web and mobile.
4 - Exemplary: Radical transparency; stakeholders have real-time access to quality health dashboards. QA provides risk-based release recommendations that stakeholders trust completely.

1 - Lagging: Over-promises readiness; says 'we're good to go' then critical bugs appear post-release. Or blocks releases indefinitely with no clear exit criteria.
2 - Developing: Provides vague readiness assessments; struggles to articulate remaining risk. Release go/no-go decisions feel arbitrary.
3 - Proficient: Balanced release gatekeeping; provides clear trade-off analysis (e.g., 'We can release with these 3 known issues — here's the risk and mitigation plan').
4 - Exemplary: Trusted quality advisor; steers release decisions with data-driven confidence levels. Stakeholders rely on QA's judgment for all go/no-go decisions.

1 - Lagging: QA tests exactly what's written in specs but misses the user intent. Tests pass but the feature doesn't solve the actual user problem.
2 - Developing: Tests cover functional requirements but lack exploratory testing for user experience quality on both web and mobile platforms.
3 - Proficient: High degree of user-centric testing; QA validates not just functionality but usability, performance, and cross-platform consistency.
4 - Exemplary: Anticipates quality risks from a user perspective; proactively suggests test scenarios that product owners hadn't considered. QA enhances the product vision.

1 - Lagging: Defensive when developers or stakeholders challenge QA findings. Slow to adjust test strategies when requirements change mid-sprint.
2 - Developing: Incorporates feedback eventually but with friction. Test plans are rigid and rarely updated based on retrospective insights.
3 - Proficient: Processes feedback constructively from all stakeholders. Iterates test strategies within the same sprint cycle based on new information.
4 - Exemplary: Feedback loops are closed instantly. QA retrospectives drive measurable process improvements every sprint.

1 - Lagging: Production escapes are patched but the same classes of bugs recur. No formal RCA process; QA doesn't learn from misses.
2 - Developing: RCAs are performed but focus on blame ('who missed it?') rather than systemic gaps ('why did our process miss it?').
3 - Proficient: High-quality RCAs for all production escapes. Every missed defect leads to a new test case and a process improvement action item.
4 - Exemplary: Blameless RCA culture. Production escapes trigger automated regression additions and process guardrails that prevent the entire category of bug from recurring.

1 - Lagging: QA is unavailable during production incidents. No clear role for QA in incident response; testers wait for someone to tell them what to do.
2 - Developing: QA responds to incidents but reactively; takes time to reproduce issues and verify fixes. No clear escalation path from QA to dev.
3 - Proficient: Clear QA role in incident response; rapid reproduction of issues, verification of hotfixes, and regression testing post-fix. MTTR benefits from QA involvement.
4 - Exemplary: QA maintains production monitoring awareness; identifies patterns in production issues that inform preventive test coverage. QA helps predict incidents before they occur.

1 - Lagging: Test environments are unstable; significant time wasted on environment-related false failures. Data inconsistency causes unreliable test results.
2 - Developing: Test environments work most of the time but periodic instability causes wasted cycles. No clear ownership of environment maintenance.
3 - Proficient: Stable, well-maintained test environments for both web and mobile. Environment issues are rare and resolved quickly. Test data is managed and refreshed systematically.
4 - Exemplary: Self-healing test environments with automated provisioning. Environment-related test failures are near-zero. QA owns and continuously improves the test infrastructure.

1 - Lagging: No formal regression process; releases go out with minimal re-testing. Previously fixed bugs frequently reappear.
2 - Developing: Basic regression suites exist but are incomplete and manually executed. Regression takes too long and delays releases.
3 - Proficient: Robust regression test suites for both web and mobile. Automated where possible; manual regression is risk-based and efficient. Release verification is thorough and timely.
4 - Exemplary: Comprehensive automated regression with intelligent test selection. Only impacted test areas run per release, drastically reducing cycle time while maintaining coverage.

1 - Lagging: Test case creation is slow; large backlogs of untested features. Reviews are rubber-stamps — no real feedback on test quality.
2 - Developing: Test cases are created on time but reviews are cursory. Test case quality varies significantly across the team of 14.
3 - Proficient: Efficient test case creation and peer review process. Reviews improve both test coverage and the reviewing tester's skills. Turnaround is within 24 hours.
4 - Exemplary: Streamlined test design process with templates and reusable components. Peer reviews are strategic — catching coverage gaps before execution begins.

1 - Lagging: Testers frequently stop work to clarify requirements. High re-work rate on test cases due to ambiguous specs.
2 - Developing: Requirements are documented but lack edge cases and acceptance criteria specific to testing. QA discovers gaps mid-execution.
3 - Proficient: 'Ready for QA' criteria are well-defined; testers have 90% of what they need upfront including test data requirements and platform-specific acceptance criteria.
4 - Exemplary: Just-in-Time test design; QA is involved in requirement refinement and story grooming. Test scenarios are co-created with developers before coding begins.

1 - Lagging: Test execution is frequently blocked by build failures, environment issues, or missing test data. Testers sit idle waiting for resolutions.
2 - Developing: Blockers occur regularly but are resolved within a day. Some automation exists but testers still spend significant time on environment setup.
3 - Proficient: Minimal execution friction; build-to-test handoff is smooth. Blocked tests are flagged immediately in ClickUp and workarounds are applied quickly.
4 - Exemplary: Near-zero execution friction. Automated build verification, self-service test environments, and intelligent test queuing keep the team productive at all times.

1 - Lagging: Unaware of where testing bottlenecks exist. Blames 'heavy workload' without data to support it.
2 - Developing: Manually tracks blockers; knows bottlenecks exist but lacks data to prove root causes or prioritize fixes.
3 - Proficient: Uses cycle time data from ClickUp to identify and fix specific bottleneck stages (e.g., 'test review takes 3x longer than execution').
4 - Exemplary: Proactive bottleneck prevention; uses metrics to predict and preempt slowdowns. Swarming techniques clear critical-path blockers instantly.

1 - Lagging: Test cases are superficial — happy path only. No systematic approach to edge cases, boundary conditions, or negative testing.
2 - Developing: Follows basic test design techniques but inconsistently. Some testers write thorough cases; others write minimal checks.
3 - Proficient: Consistent application of test design techniques (equivalence partitioning, boundary analysis, state transition) across the team. Both web and mobile test suites are comprehensive.
4 - Exemplary: Advanced test design practices including combinatorial testing, model-based testing, and risk-based prioritization. Test suites are optimized for maximum defect detection with minimum cases.

1 - Lagging: No traceability between requirements and test cases. Tribal knowledge is required to understand test coverage.
2 - Developing: Basic test documentation exists but is inconsistent. Traceability matrix is incomplete or outdated.
3 - Proficient: Clear traceability from requirements to test cases to defects. Test documentation is self-explanatory; any QA team member can pick up and execute.
4 - Exemplary: Living test documentation that auto-updates with requirement changes. Full traceability with coverage reports that stakeholders can self-serve.

1 - Lagging: Test reviews are rubber stamps. Junior testers receive no meaningful guidance on improving their craft.
2 - Developing: Reviews catch surface-level issues but miss strategic coverage gaps. Mentorship is limited to senior testers occasionally helping juniors.
3 - Proficient: Rigorous peer reviews that improve both test quality and tester skills. Structured mentorship pairing across the team of 14.
4 - Exemplary: Culture of quality; reviews are seen as strategic alignment opportunities. Senior testers are developing the next generation of QA leaders within the team.

1 - Lagging: No automation; or automation exists but is so brittle and unmaintained that it provides negative value (false failures waste time).
2 - Developing: Automation covers some happy paths but maintenance burden is high. Flaky tests erode team confidence in automation.
3 - Proficient: Healthy automation suite with clear ownership. Flaky test rate is low. Automation covers critical paths for both web and mobile platforms.
4 - Exemplary: Strategic automation roadmap; the team continuously improves automation ROI. Test automation code is reviewed with the same rigor as production code.

1 - Lagging: No awareness of testing gaps. No backlog of 'tests we should have but don't.' Coverage blind spots are invisible.
2 - Developing: Test debt is acknowledged verbally but not tracked. Known gaps exist but aren't prioritized against new test work.
3 - Proficient: Clear 'test debt' backlog in ClickUp; gaps are categorized by risk and platform (web vs mobile). Team knows exactly where coverage is thin.
4 - Exemplary: Test debt is quantified with risk scores. Live dashboard shows coverage health across all applications; debt items have clear retirement timelines.

1 - Lagging: 0% of sprint capacity goes to improving existing test coverage; team only writes tests for new features.
2 - Developing: Test debt is only addressed during slow sprints or after a production escape forces action.
3 - Proficient: Consistent 15-20% allocation of each sprint to improving existing test coverage, updating stale tests, and expanding automation.
4 - Exemplary: Strategic 'test improvement sprints' are part of the roadmap. The team treats test maintenance as a first-class citizen alongside new feature testing.

1 - Lagging: High-risk areas of the application have the least test coverage. No awareness of which untested areas pose the greatest production risk.
2 - Developing: Reactive approach; only improves coverage for areas that have already caused production incidents.
3 - Proficient: Proactively identifies high-risk areas (payment flows, authentication, data sync between web and mobile) and ensures they have the strongest coverage.
4 - Exemplary: Risk heatmap drives all test investment decisions. Zero 'critical' coverage gaps exist; the team retires risk before it manifests.

1 - Lagging: New features ship without adequate test coverage. 'We'll add tests later' is the norm (and 'later' never comes).
2 - Developing: Team knows tests should be comprehensive but leadership pressure to ship fast erodes standards. Definition of Done is loosely enforced.
3 - Proficient: Strong Definition of Done requires test coverage before any feature is marked complete. New test debt is the exception, not the rule.
4 - Exemplary: 'Leave it better than you found it' culture. Every feature delivery improves overall test coverage. The team proactively refactors test suites alongside new work.

1 - Lagging: No formal 1:1s. Junior QA feel stuck; skill development is entirely self-driven. High turnover among promising testers.
2 - Developing: Irregular feedback; growth plans exist on paper but aren't actively followed. Learning happens only when project demands force it.
3 - Proficient: Structured growth plans for every tester on the team of 14. Regular 1:1s focus on career development. Clear progression path from manual tester to automation/lead roles.
4 - Exemplary: Force multiplier effect; senior QA are actively groomed for leadership. Clear internal promotion path. The team produces future QA leads for the organization.

1 - Lagging: Tribal knowledge culture; if one tester leaves, critical platform/application knowledge is lost. Onboarding new QA takes months.
2 - Developing: Occasional knowledge sharing sessions but documentation is sparse. New team members rely heavily on shadowing.
3 - Proficient: Robust internal wiki with platform-specific test guides (web browsers, Android devices, iOS versions). Regular 'show & tell' sessions. Cross-training is standard.
4 - Exemplary: Open knowledge culture; the team maintains living documentation that makes onboarding a new QA engineer fast and self-service. Team contributes to org-wide quality practices.

1 - Lagging: Team relies solely on manual testing. No investment in learning automation, performance testing, or security testing fundamentals.
2 - Developing: Some team members are learning automation but only when forced by project needs. No structured upskilling plan.
3 - Proficient: Proactive skill gap analysis. Training in automation frameworks, API testing, performance testing is planned and executed. Team skills match the testing demands of 2026.
4 - Exemplary: Future-proofing; the team explores AI-assisted testing, visual regression testing, and other emerging QA practices. The team is 6-12 months ahead of industry trends.

1 - Lagging: Rigid silos; web testers can't test mobile and vice versa. The team of 14 operates as two isolated groups.
2 - Developing: Some testers can work across platforms but struggle with the nuances (device-specific behaviors, browser quirks, OS differences).
3 - Proficient: Most testers are T-shaped: deep expertise in one platform (web or mobile) with working knowledge of the other. Team can flex across platforms during crunch time.
4 - Exemplary: High agility; any tester can cover any platform competently. The team self-organizes based on release priority without supervisor micro-management.

1 - Lagging: Blame culture; testers are afraid to report 'it might be a bug' findings for fear of being wrong. Developers dismiss QA concerns.
2 - Developing: People speak up occasionally but usually only confirm bugs when they're 100% certain. Borderline issues go unreported.
3 - Proficient: Team feels safe to report uncertain findings, challenge developer pushback, and flag quality concerns without fear of retribution.
4 - Exemplary: High vulnerability leadership; mistakes (missed bugs, false positives) are treated as learning opportunities. Team takes calculated risks in exploratory testing.

1 - Lagging: Team feels like 'gate-checkers'; no understanding of how their quality work impacts end users or the business.
2 - Developing: Aware of company goals but doesn't see how finding bug #4,721 moves the needle.
3 - Proficient: Clear connection between QA work and user satisfaction. Team understands that their vigilance directly protects the company's reputation and user trust.
4 - Exemplary: Mission-driven quality culture; the team proactively suggests improvements that align with business outcomes, not just bug counts.

1 - Lagging: High regrettable attrition; testers leave due to burnout, boredom (repetitive manual testing), or lack of growth opportunities.
2 - Developing: Turnover is average but quiet quitting is visible — testers do the minimum. Low enthusiasm in sprint ceremonies.
3 - Proficient: Low attrition; high team engagement. QA engineers stay because of the culture, growth opportunities, and meaningful work across web and mobile platforms.
4 - Exemplary: The QA team is a talent magnet; internal transfers into this team are common. Team members are proud advocates of the QA function.

1 - Lagging: Recognition is non-existent or only given when a major production bug is caught last-minute (rewarding firefighting, not prevention).
2 - Developing: Generic 'good job' acknowledgments. Preventive quality work (thorough test design, automation investment) goes unnoticed.
3 - Proficient: Regular, specific recognition. Accomplishments like improved automation coverage, reduced production escapes, and mentorship contributions are celebrated.
4 - Exemplary: Tailored recognition; the supervisor understands what motivates each of the 14 team members (growth, visibility, flexibility) and acts accordingly.

1 - Lagging: No centralized QA knowledge base. Test strategies, device configurations, and platform-specific gotchas live in individual heads.
2 - Developing: Documentation exists in scattered Google Docs but is stale and hard to find. No single source of truth for QA knowledge.
3 - Proficient: Well-maintained QA wiki in Google Workspace with platform-specific guides, common defect patterns, and environment setup instructions. Documentation is part of the Definition of Done.
4 - Exemplary: Self-healing knowledge base; documentation is continuously updated. New team members can onboard and be productive within a week using docs alone.

1 - Lagging: QA operates in isolation; quality trends from one product (web vs mobile) don't inform testing strategies for others.
2 - Developing: Occasional insights are shared between web and mobile QA tracks but no systematic cross-pollination with dev teams.
3 - Proficient: Regular quality trend reports shared with all dev divisions (Frontend, Backend, Mobile, Hardware, GameDev). Defect patterns in one area inform testing in others.
4 - Exemplary: QA is the connective tissue of the organization. Cross-division defect pattern analysis leads to preventive improvements across all teams.

1 - Lagging: Stagnant testing approach; team uses the same methods and tools they used years ago. No experimentation with new testing approaches.
2 - Developing: Innovation happens only in crisis; new tools are adopted only when existing ones completely fail to meet needs.
3 - Proficient: Dedicated innovation time for exploring new testing tools, AI-assisted testing, visual regression, performance testing approaches. Clear process for evaluating and adopting new methods.
4 - Exemplary: High failure velocity; the team rapidly experiments with and evaluates new QA techniques. 1-2 significant process improvements per quarter come from experimentation.

1 - Lagging: The team only consumes existing internal practices; no awareness of industry QA trends or external best practices.
2 - Developing: Aware of industry trends (shift-left, AI testing, continuous testing) but always 12-18 months behind adoption.
3 - Proficient: QA practices are current with industry standards. The team actively evaluates external best practices and adopts what fits.
4 - Exemplary: Thought leadership; the QA team's practices are referenced by other divisions. Team members participate in QA communities and bring insights back.

1 - Lagging: Team sees QA as 'finding bugs' with no connection to business outcomes. 'I'm just running test cases.'
2 - Developing: Leader mentions quality goals in meetings but it doesn't influence daily testing priorities or team motivation.
3 - Proficient: Every test cycle is framed with business context. Team understands why this release matters and what quality means for the user, not just the defect count.
4 - Exemplary: Quality is the north star; the team rejects shortcuts that compromise user experience. QA is seen as a business enabler, not a cost center.

1 - Lagging: Passive; waits for dev teams to tell QA what to test. No proactive quality strategy.
2 - Developing: Suggests small testing improvements but waits for permission to implement broader quality initiatives.
3 - Proficient: Regularly proposes quality initiatives (e.g., shift-left testing adoption, automated smoke suites, cross-platform test standardization) that solve real business problems.
4 - Exemplary: Proactive intrapreneurship; identifies quality gaps that leadership hadn't seen and proposes solutions that deliver measurable business value.

1 - Lagging: Measures success by number of test cases executed or bugs found — vanity metrics that don't reflect quality outcomes.
2 - Developing: Tracks test metrics but struggles to quantify how QA prevents business loss or improves user satisfaction.
3 - Proficient: Success is defined by business-aligned KPIs: production escape rate, defect detection effectiveness, release confidence score, mean time to detect.
4 - Exemplary: Obsessed with ROI; redirects testing effort away from low-risk areas toward high-impact areas even if it means fewer total test cases.

1 - Lagging: Blames development teams for poor quality or management for unrealistic timelines when releases have quality issues.
2 - Developing: Passively accepts the testing scope but doesn't advocate for QA's role in the broader product quality story.
3 - Proficient: Acts as a bridge; translates quality data into narratives that motivate both the QA team and development partners.
4 - Exemplary: Culture of radical ownership; the QA team feels co-responsible for product quality, not just responsible for 'finding what developers missed.'

1 - Lagging: All bugs are treated equally; no severity triage. Critical issues sit alongside cosmetic ones in an undifferentiated backlog.
2 - Developing: Basic triage exists but severity assignments are inconsistent. High-priority bugs sometimes wait behind lower-priority ones.
3 - Proficient: Disciplined triage process; bugs are categorized by severity, platform impact, and user exposure. Critical/high bugs are fast-tracked with clear SLAs.
4 - Exemplary: Intelligent triage using defect pattern analysis and user impact data. The team anticipates which bugs will have the highest business impact and escalates accordingly.

1 - Lagging: Tests everything equally; no risk assessment informs testing depth. Time is wasted on low-risk areas while high-risk flows are under-tested.
2 - Developing: Identifies high-risk areas but lacks a formal framework for adjusting test depth based on risk. Gut-feeling drives decisions.
3 - Proficient: Formal risk-based testing approach. Test effort is proportional to risk (likelihood x impact). High-risk flows get exhaustive testing; low-risk areas get smoke coverage.
4 - Exemplary: Predictive risk modeling; uses historical defect data and code change analysis to dynamically adjust testing focus per release.

1 - Lagging: Makes invisible quality decisions; team and stakeholders don't know what wasn't tested or what risks remain in a release.
2 - Developing: Explains testing gaps after the fact, usually only when a production issue forces the conversation.
3 - Proficient: Clearly articulates trade-offs: 'We tested X thoroughly but Y only had smoke coverage due to time constraints — here's the residual risk.'
4 - Exemplary: Decision journaling; documents testing trade-offs for every release to build institutional wisdom about risk acceptance.

1 - Lagging: Deflects blame when production escapes occur; points fingers at developers or unclear requirements.
2 - Developing: Takes responsibility after investigation but is defensive during the initial heat of a production incident.
3 - Proficient: The buck stops here; provides calm, data-driven assessment during production incidents and takes ownership of the QA gap that allowed the escape.
4 - Exemplary: Radical accountability; uses production escapes as catalysts to overhaul testing processes and prevent entire categories of future escapes.

1 - Lagging: Communication is too technical or too late. Stakeholders are confused by defect density charts and don't know if the release is safe.
2 - Developing: Reports are sent regularly but lack 'so what?' insights. Stakeholders still have to ask 'are we safe to release?'
3 - Proficient: Proactive, audience-appropriate quality reporting. Translates test results into business language: risk level, confidence score, and recommended actions.
4 - Exemplary: Executive-level quality narrative. Stakeholders don't just get updates; they get strategic quality insights that inform business decisions.

1 - Lagging: Radio silence during critical test phases. Developers and PMs wait in the dark for QA results or bug confirmations.
2 - Developing: Responds when asked but rarely proactively flags emerging quality concerns. Dev teams are surprised by late bug reports.
3 - Proficient: Predictable communication cadence. Blockers and quality concerns are flagged in ClickUp before they impact release timelines.
4 - Exemplary: Instant transparency; automated test result notifications and quality dashboards keep all stakeholders informed in real-time.

1 - Lagging: Information hoarding; test strategies and platform-specific knowledge stay in a few testers' heads. Team reinvents the wheel constantly.
2 - Developing: Documentation exists but is unorganized. The Google Workspace has scattered docs with no clear structure or ownership.
3 - Proficient: Documentation is a Definition of Done for QA. Active knowledge base maintenance. Regular internal demos and cross-platform learning sessions.
4 - Exemplary: Living documentation culture. The team enjoys sharing knowledge. QA playbooks are self-explanatory and continuously improved.

1 - Lagging: Defensive when developers challenge bug validity. Stakeholder feedback about QA process is dismissed or ignored.
2 - Developing: Takes feedback but acts on it slowly. Developer-QA feedback is a one-way street (QA reports bugs, devs push back, nothing improves).
3 - Proficient: Actively solicits feedback from developers, PMs, and stakeholders. QA retrospectives drive real improvements to the testing process.
4 - Exemplary: Radical candor. Creates a space where developers and QA collaborate on quality rather than operating as adversaries. Stakeholder voice is reflected in QA strategy.

1 - Lagging: Reactive; only addresses skill gaps after a major quality miss. Relies on hiring to fill capability gaps rather than developing existing team.
2 - Developing: Identifies gaps but lacks structured training. QA skills are learned on the fly under sprint pressure.
3 - Proficient: Regular skill audits for all 14 team members. Clear upskilling paths: manual to automation, functional to performance/security testing. Training tied to 12-month roadmap.
4 - Exemplary: Future-proofing; anticipates industry shifts (AI-assisted testing, codeless automation, shift-left practices) and builds team capability before the business needs it.

1 - Lagging: 'We've always tested it this way.' QA workflows are rigid; retrospective feedback is ignored.
2 - Developing: Improvements are sporadic patches to fix immediate pain points rather than systemic upgrades to the QA process.
3 - Proficient: Regular workflow audits via retrospectives. At least one meaningful process improvement per month: faster test cycles, better defect triage, improved automation coverage.
4 - Exemplary: Self-optimizing QA system. Constant experimentation with tools and methods results in measurable quality and efficiency gains quarter over quarter.

1 - Lagging: Heavy reliance on manual testing for everything. No automation strategy; all 14 testers execute tests by hand.
2 - Developing: Basic automation exists but is fragile, poorly maintained, and covers only a fraction of critical paths. Viewed as 'nice to have.'
3 - Proficient: Active automation strategy. Manual toil is progressively replaced with reliable automated checks. Automation covers critical regression for both web and mobile platforms.
4 - Exemplary: Hyper-automation. AI-assisted test generation, intelligent test selection, and self-healing test scripts. The team focuses on exploratory and strategic testing while automation handles regression.

1 - Lagging: No time allocated for learning. QA team members who want to learn new testing techniques must do so on their own time.
2 - Developing: Encourages learning in theory but sprint pressure always wins. No budget or time allocated for courses, certifications, or conferences.
3 - Proficient: Dedicated learning time (e.g., 10% of sprint capacity). Team actively shares course completions, certifications, and testing technique discoveries.
4 - Exemplary: Thought leadership culture. The QA team is a learning machine. Knowledge sharing is systematic and the team contributes to the broader QA community.

1 - Lagging: Team actively resists new processes or tools; shadow work (doing it the old way) is common among the 14 members.
2 - Developing: Adoption is slow and forced; testers comply only when the supervisor is watching. New tools are used minimally.
3 - Proficient: 80%+ of the team adopts new workflows within the target timeframe. Changes to QA process are embraced, not just tolerated.
4 - Exemplary: Rapid viral adoption. The team doesn't just adopt changes; they improve upon them. QA process innovations spread to other teams.

1 - Lagging: Dismissive of concerns; 'just do it' approach. Team frustration builds silently.
2 - Developing: Listens to concerns but lacks a plan to address them. Resistance lingers and slows down improvements.
3 - Proficient: Proactively identifies resistors among the 14 team members. Holds open forums to address fears and clarify the 'why' behind QA process changes.
4 - Exemplary: Psychological safety around change. Resistance is treated as valuable feedback. Skeptics are converted into change champions.

1 - Lagging: Communicates changes via impersonal messages; no context or vision for where QA is headed.
2 - Developing: Explains changes but fails to show 'what's in it for the team' — how new tools or processes will make their work better.
3 - Proficient: Connects QA changes to both business goals and team benefits (e.g., 'This automation framework will eliminate repetitive regression work and free you for exploratory testing').
4 - Exemplary: Master storytelling. Creates a compelling vision for the QA function's future that makes evolution feel exciting, not threatening.

1 - Lagging: Change is chaotic; the team of 14 feels whiplashed by frequent, uncoordinated shifts in testing approach or tooling.
2 - Developing: Changes are structured but frequent enough to cause fatigue. Testers can't build mastery before the next change arrives.
3 - Proficient: Balances necessary evolution with stability anchors. Core QA processes remain constant while specific tools and techniques evolve.
4 - Exemplary: High-frequency agility. The team is so well-practiced in adapting that process changes feel like minor course corrections, not disruptions.

1 - Lagging: Low energy; clock-in/clock-out culture across the team of 14. QA is seen as the 'boring' part of software development.
2 - Developing: Functional morale; the team does the work but lacks excitement or pride in the QA craft.
3 - Proficient: High engagement; team celebrates quality wins together (zero production escapes, successful releases, automation milestones). Strong team identity.
4 - Exemplary: Contagious enthusiasm. The QA division has a distinct positive energy. High resilience — the team stays motivated even during high-pressure release cycles.

1 - Lagging: Gatekeeping; supervisor makes all testing decisions and assigns all work. No autonomy for the team.
2 - Developing: Mentorship is ad-hoc; only top performers get attention. Development of the 14 members is a checkbox exercise.
3 - Proficient: Structured mentorship at all levels. Delegates real authority — senior testers own test strategy for their platform, not just task execution.
4 - Exemplary: Talent incubator. The supervisor is known for developing strong QA professionals. Team members are promoted to leadership roles across the organization.

1 - Lagging: Never admits when QA misses something. Team feels they must be 'perfect' to avoid blame.
2 - Developing: Shares minor issues but maintains a controlling facade. Team hesitates to flag potential false positives or uncertain findings.
3 - Proficient: Admits when QA processes fail. Builds trust through honesty. Team feels safe reporting ambiguous findings without fear of looking incompetent.
4 - Exemplary: Radical authenticity. Leads with vulnerability, creating a space where the team takes calculated risks in exploratory testing and speaks up about systemic issues.

1 - Lagging: Sees testers only for their current role; pigeonholes manual testers as 'manual-only' and automation testers as 'just scripters.'
2 - Developing: Recognizes hard work but doesn't actively stretch people into new domains (e.g., manual tester interested in automation, tester interested in DevOps).
3 - Proficient: Identifies hidden gems and provides stretch assignments. Helps manual testers transition to automation, testers interested in leadership get project ownership.
4 - Exemplary: Pathfinder leadership. Sees potential in team members they don't see in themselves. Creates growth opportunities that transform career trajectories.

1 - Lagging: Follows the same testing playbook even when it's clearly missing bugs. No experimentation with new approaches.
2 - Developing: Tries new testing techniques only when existing ones completely fail. Innovation is reactive and rare.
3 - Proficient: Actively encourages safe-to-fail experiments. Regularly implements creative testing approaches (chaos testing, AI-generated test data, crowd-sourced testing for edge cases).
4 - Exemplary: Transformative innovation. Creates testing approaches that catch categories of bugs the team didn't know existed. QA methodology becomes a competitive advantage.

1 - Lagging: Escalates testing blockers immediately without attempting to find workarounds. Test cycles stall at the first sign of friction.
2 - Developing: Resolves blockers linearly (brute force). Solutions involve 'just wait for the fix' rather than creative workarounds.
3 - Proficient: High efficiency in finding workarounds. Can re-prioritize test execution, use mocks, or shift test focus to keep the team productive when blockers arise.
4 - Exemplary: Obstacle transmutation. Uses testing blockers as catalysts to redesign the build-to-test handoff process, permanently removing friction for the whole organization.

1 - Lagging: Punishes 'creative' testing. Team is afraid to go off-script from documented test cases for fear of wasting time.
2 - Developing: Allows exploratory testing in theory but doesn't allocate time or reward insights from it. Experimentation happens 'off the clock.'
3 - Proficient: Allocates dedicated time for exploratory testing and tool experimentation. Celebrates discoveries — both bugs found and process insights gained.
4 - Exemplary: Institutionalized curiosity. The QA team is a laboratory for testing innovation. Creative approaches are part of the culture, not an exception.

1 - Lagging: Requests more headcount as the only solution to testing capacity issues. Can't see how to do more with the existing team of 14.
2 - Developing: Uses existing resources but struggles when multiple releases compete for QA time. Defaults to overtime.
3 - Proficient: Does more with less. Finds creative ways to use automation, risk-based testing, and cross-training to handle peak loads without burnout.
4 - Exemplary: Mastery of constraints. Solves QA capacity challenges by simplifying test strategies and leveraging the full breadth of 14 team members' combined skills.
